figures.caption: Fig. 1. Bratu equation: simulated solution fields using P = 25 (top row) and P = 225 (bottom row) for NiL-N, NiL-Q, LiL-N and LiL-Q from left to right.
Fig. 2. Bratu equation: convergence histories for various methods using networks with P ∈ {25, 100, 225} parameters.
Fig. 3. Bratu equation: convergence histories organized by method.

Fig. 4. Bratu equation: comparison of linearized and nonlinear residual norms for LiL-Q at P ∈ {25, 100, 225}. The asymptotic agreement between the two quantities corroborates the convergence analysis of Section 3.5.
Table 2: Burgers equation: iteration counts and runtimes. An asterisk (*) indicates no convergence.
Fig. 5. Burgers equation: convergence histories organized by method. Each panel shows all five basis sizes for a single formulation.
Fig. 6. Burgers equation: comparison of linearized and nonlinear residual norms for LiL-Q at P ∈ {25, 100, 225, 400, 625}.

Table 3: LiL configurations for the viscous Burgers equation (P = 625, ν = 0.1), ordered by final MSE. Each row specifies the spatial and temporal activations whose tensor product forms the two-dimensional network.
Fig. 7. Burgers equation: predicted solution fields at P = 625.
Fig. 8. Convergence histories for eight LiL configurations on the viscous Burgers equation (P = 625).
Fig. 9. Buckley-Leverett (viscous flow, Ng = 0): convergence histories organized by method. Each panel shows all four basis sizes for a single formulation.
Fig. 10. Buckley-Leverett (viscous flow, Ng = 0): comparison of linearized and nonlinear residual norms for LiL-Q at P ∈ {64, 256, 576, 1,024}.
Fig. 11. Buckley-Leverett (gravity-influenced flow, Ng = −5): convergence histories organized by method. The NiL-Q panel exhibits order-of-magnitude loss spikes not observed in any other experiment.
Fig. 12. Buckley-Leverett (gravity-influenced flow, Ng = −5): comparison of linearized and nonlinear residual norms for LiL-Q at P ∈ {64, 256, 576, 1,024}.
Fig. 13. Buckley-Leverett solutions at P = 1,024: NiL-N (left) and LiL-Q (right) for viscous displacement (top) and gravityinfluenced displacement (bottom).

Fig. 14. Linear elasticity at P = 50: exact displacement fields (left), LiL predictions (center), and pointwise absolute errors (right). Errors are at O(10−15) throughout the domain, consistent with machine-precision recovery.
Fig. 15. Kovasznay flow at P = 1,875: exact fields (left), LiL-Q predictions (center), and pointwise absolute errors (right) for u, v, and p.
Table 9: Kovasznay flow (Re = 40): Summary of published PINN solver results. Each row presents a reported configuration from the corresponding reference. A dash indicates unreported data.
Fig. 16. Beltrami flow at t = 1: exact fields (left), LiL-Q predictions (middle), and pointwise absolute errors (right) for u, v, w, and p.

Table 12: Training configurations for the single-phase Darcy flow experiments. Three separate networks are used for h∗, u∗, and v∗ in the NiL formulation; the reported parameter count is the total across all three.
Fig. 17. Permeability fields (log10 K in mD) for the four test cases, with heterogeneity contrast increasing from left to right.
Table 13: PDE residual MSE and solve times for single-phase Darcy flow. LiL: 32 Fourier modes per direction (P = 3,169). NiL: 2 hidden layers, 32 neurons (P = 3,555), 150,000 iterations.

Table 14: Condition number κ(A(k)) of the LiL-Q collocation matrix across the experiments. Columns report the parameter range tested, κ at the smallest and largest parameter counts (final-iterate values), the across-iteration stability factor maxk κ/mink κ after the first outer iterate.
Fig. 18. Pressure field solutions for (a) FVM (b) NiL and (c) LiL across the four permeability realizations.
